data.title: RBA cuts its cash rate by 25 bps for first time since 2020, in-line with market expectations
data.description:
The Reserve Bank of Australia reduced its cash rate by 25 basis points to 4.1%, in line with expectations. This marks the first-rate cut since November 2020, driven by easing inflationary pressures.

The RBA also trimmed the interest rate on Exchange Settlement balances by 25bps to 4.0%.

The central bank is confident that inflation is settling within its 2-3% target range, attributed to higher interest rates balancing demand and supply.

However, the RBA warns of an uncertain economic outlook, slower private demand rebound, and uncertainty over household spending recovery. The board remains cautious about further policy easing ahead of a federal election.

Traders are now awaiting comments from Governor Michelle Bullock for further insights into the decision.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index dropped 0.4% to approach 8,500 on Tuesday, marking its second consecutive session of losses. The Australian dollar fell below $0.635 on Tuesday, reversing earlier gains as investors digested the Reserve Bank of Australia's latest policy announcement.

ETFs: (EWA), (FXA), (FLAU).

More on Australia

  • Australia's manufacturing downturn deepens in December
  • Australia's jobless rate falls to 8-month low of 3.9% in November
  • Reserve Bank of Australia holds cash rate steady at 4.35%, adopts mildly dovish stance
  • Australia's economy grows at weaker-than-expected pace in Q3; services activity remains in expansion
  • RBA Minutes: Discussed scenarios that could warrant rate cuts; retains restrictive monetary policy

data.title: Tuesday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
8:30 AM Empire State Mfg Survey

The Empire State Manufacturing Index rates the relative level of general business conditions New York state. The NY Empire State Manufacturing Index is expected to edge lower 0.5 in February after a fall of 12.6 in January.

10:00 AM Housing Market Index

The National Association of Home Builders (NAHB) Housing Market Index (HMI) is a survey of 900 home builders who rate the relative level of current and future single-family home sales. The Housing Market Index is expected to hold steady at 47 in February.

10:20 AM Fed's Daly Speech

San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly speaks and participates in a moderated discussion before the 2025 Conference for Community Bankers hosted by the American Bankers Association.

1:00 PM Fed's Barr: "Artificial Intelligence in the Economy and Financial Stability"

Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr speaks on "Artificial Intelligence in the Economy and Financial Stability" before the Council on Foreign Relations.

4:00 PM Treasury International Capital

The Treasury International Capital reporting system is the U.S. government's source of data on capital flows into and out of the United States, excluding direct investment, and the resulting levels of cross-border claims and liabilities.

data.title: Deterra outlines $0.09 dividend and signals further revenue growth with Thacker Pass catalysts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Deterra Royalties Limited (OTCPK:DETRF) Q2 2025

Management View

  • The CEO, Julian Andrews, highlighted record volumes from key producing assets, including Mining Area C, which benefited from BHP's South Flank expansion reaching nameplate capacity. Additionally, the company reported initial revenue contributions from newly acquired gold offtake contracts, which performed strongly during the period.
  • Andrews noted significant progress in development assets, with Thacker Pass securing funding through a U.S. Department of Energy loan and General Motors investments, enabling Phase 1 of the project. The updated report from Lithium Americas also indicated increased reserves and a doubled production capacity for the asset.
  • He emphasized that the integration of the Trident portfolio is now complete, outperforming expectations in financial contribution and development progress. The company continues to evaluate further value-accretive investment opportunities.
  • CFO Jason Clifton reported $106 million in underlying EBITDA for the period, reflecting a 7% decline mainly due to reduced iron ore prices, partially offset by higher volumes and new revenue streams from gold offtake contracts.
  • Clifton confirmed the declaration of a fully franked $0.09 per share dividend, representing a 74.5% payout ratio of net profit after tax.

Outlook

  • Julian Andrews stated that Deterra's strategy focuses on shareholder value through project development and disciplined investment in high-quality, long-life assets. He mentioned upcoming catalysts, including a final notice to proceed on Thacker Pass and Avino’s La Preciosa silver mine, which is expected to begin processing later this year.
  • Andrews reiterated the company’s long-term focus on investment opportunities in established mining jurisdictions and commodities like base metals, bulk commodities, and energy transition materials.
  • He noted that while Mining Area C production rates may see incremental volume growth, the company remains well-positioned for further portfolio diversification.

Financial Results

  • Underlying EBITDA for the half-year was $106 million, driven by a mix of lower iron ore prices, higher Mining Area C volumes, and $7 million in contributions from gold offtake contracts consolidated from September 2024.
  • Net profit was $63.9 million, and an interim dividend of $0.09 per share was declared. Adjusted net profit accounting for one-off items was $68.5 million.
  • The MAC royalty revenue declined 12% year-over-year, primarily due to a 22% drop in realized iron ore prices, partially offset by increased sales volumes.
  • Gold offtake revenue showed strong growth, supported by resumed operations at Los Filos and Greenstone, contributing significantly to offset Trident-related costs.
  • The company reported net debt of $308 million as of December 2024, with an average interest rate of 5.84%. Jason Clifton affirmed that the balance sheet remains strong, with ample liquidity and available credit facilities.

Q&A

  • Tom Prendiville, Canaccord, asked about the restructuring of the business development team and the increased focus on North American opportunities. Julian Andrews explained that the presence in North America reflects the maturity of the royalty and streaming sector there, with Adam Davidson now leading efforts from Denver.
  • Prendiville followed up regarding the potential divestment of outperforming gold offtakes. Andrews stated that while the gold assets are performing well and generating cash flow, they remain non-core, and divestment would be considered if a favorable opportunity arises.
  • Glyn Lawcock, Barrenjoey, questioned the payout ratio and capital structure. Julian Andrews and Jason Clifton emphasized the company’s comfort with its current debt levels and liquidity, underlining that gold offtake revenue alone services the Trident acquisition debt. Andrews noted that equity could be an option for substantial future acquisitions.
  • Paul McTaggart, Citigroup, raised concerns about the Thacker Pass project’s scale and funding. Andrews reassured that the project is well-supported by DOE loans and GM investments, highlighting its long-life, tier-1 characteristics and recent reserves/resource upgrades.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly positive, focusing on gold offtakes’ outperformance, strategic restructuring, and Thacker Pass progress but raised concerns about capital structure and future funding.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, particularly about the Trident integration, Thacker Pass funding, and optionality in the portfolio. However, responses to potential asset divestments and future acquisitions were more cautious, with Andrews emphasizing evaluation on a case-by-case basis.
  • Compared to prepared remarks, management was more reserved during the Q&A, particularly when addressing future investment and capital management strategies.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • No comparison to previous quarter available.

Risks and Concerns

  • Analysts expressed concerns about the debt levels post-Trident acquisition and potential working capital swings.
  • Questions about the feasibility of Thacker Pass targets were raised, particularly regarding funding and operational complexity.
  • Management emphasized the strength of its balance sheet and funding arrangements but acknowledged the importance of maintaining flexibility for future investments.

Final Takeaway

Deterra Royalties delivered a strong performance this quarter, driven by record Mining Area C volumes and gold offtake revenue. The integration of the Trident portfolio has exceeded expectations, while the Thacker Pass project presents significant growth potential with secured funding and increased resource estimates. Management remains focused on disciplined investments and shareholder returns, supported by a stable balance sheet and a fully franked $0.09 dividend.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Southwest expands cost-cutting by announcing 15% reduction in corporate overhead workforce
data.description:
After announcing a couple of decisions in January, including reducing pilot head count at a couple of bases and pause on hiring, Southwest Airlines (NYSE:LUV) on Monday announced slashing about 15% of its corporate jobs or 1,750 people in cost saving measure.
The workforce reduction of approximately 1,750 roles is focused almost entirely on corporate overhead and leadership positions and represents approximately 15% of corporate positions, including senior leadership and directors. Eleven senior leadership positions (Vice President and above), also representing 15% of the Company's senior management committee, will be eliminated as part of this restructuring. Separations will be substantially complete by the end of second quarter 2025.
The Company estimates partial year 2025 savings to be approximately $210 million and full-year 2026 savings to be approximately $300 million. These savings exclude an expected one-time charge in first quarter 2025 in the range of $60 million to $80 million, substantially all of which is related to severance payments and post-employment benefits, which the Company expects to treat as a special item. The Company will continue to report on additional cost-savings actions as 2025 progresses.
"This decision is unprecedented in our 53-year history, and change requires that we make difficult decisions. We are at a pivotal moment as we transform Southwest Airlines into a leaner, faster, and more agile organization," said Bob Jordan, Chief Executive Officer.
In November the Dallas-based airline offered buyouts and extended leaves of absence to airport workers to avoid what it called “overstaffing in certain locations,” which it blamed on a shortage of new planes from Boeing.
The company had paused corporate hiring and suspended most of its summer internships in January.
The carrier also reduced pilot positions by 270 at its Denver and Atlanta hubs as part of the carrier’s cost-cutting and aircraft efficiency efforts.
Southwest was a profit machine for its first 50 years — it never suffered a full-year loss until the pandemic crushed air travel in 2020. Since then, the company has been more profitable than American Airlines but far less so than Delta Air Lines and United Airlines.
Southwest (NYSE:LUV) reported mixed Q4 results last month, wherein the carrier beat EPS by 10 cents, while missed revenue consensus by $50 million. The carrier’s shares are down 10% over the past 12 months, while United are up more than 143% and shares in Delta Airlines and American Airlines have gained about 60% and 7%, respectively.
Source: Press Release

data.title: Tech execs admit DeepSeek ramps up U.S.- China competition, but see limited threat to OpenAI
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence lab DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) displays with its technological advancements that the “game is on” when it comes to U.S.-Sino competition on AI, several tech executives told CNBC on the sidelines of France’s Artificial Intelligence Action Summit.

The Chinese AI start-up, which sent shockwaves across the tech industry last month, demonstrates that China can’t be counted out as a serious player when it comes to AI innovation, tech leaders admitted.

DeepSeek managed to rattle Silicon Valley last month, as it gained global attention after it released its open-source AI model, DeepSeek-R1.

The startup detailed in a paper how LLMs can be built on bootstrapped budgets and improved without human supervision, sparking a frenzied debate on whether well-funded U.S. companies can maintain their technical lead

Chris Lehane, chief global affairs officer at OpenAI, told CNBC that DeepSeek’s advanced, low-cost model confirms there is a “very real competition between U.S.-led, small D democratic AI and CCP [Chinese Communist Party] China-led autocratic, authoritarian AI.”

“For us, what DeepSeek really reinforces and reaffirms is that there is this very real competition with very real stakes,” Lehane added.

Reid Hoffman, co-founder of LinkedIn told CNBC on Monday that DeepSeek’s new model is “a big deal in showing that the game is on.”

“The competition is afoot with China,” Hoffman said, adding that DeepSeek’s R1 is “a credible, actionable model.”

However, the AI executives were convinced that DeepSeek does not pose any meaningful threat to the businesses of AI labs such as OpenAI and Anthropic.

“Most of the market fear around DeepSeek is in fact misplaced,” Hoffman said, “It still requires large models — it was distilled from large models.”

“I think the short answer everyone should take is: game on — but large models still really matter,” he added.
